Event: David Fairchild was born in Lansing, Michigan. His parents were George Fairchild, who was president of Kansas State University and the Agricultural College of Michigan, and Charlotte Pearl Halsted Fairchild, who founded the Domestic Science Club of Kansas State University.
Source: The Food Explorer: Book Ch.1; The world was my Garden; Kansas State University library

Event: After studying at the College of Agriculture at Kansas State University, Fairchild joined the USDA. At that time, however, his work was not related to plant exploration or introductions, but rather to plant pathology.

After obtaining a Smithsonian fellowship in Naples, Fairchild departs for Europe on the SS Fulda. On board, he has a brief encounter with Barbour Lathrop, who will later become his patron and expedition partner.
As Fairchild studies at the Naples Zoological Station, Lathrop offers him $1,000 USD per year to accompany him on his travels.

On December 17 in Corsica, Fairchild attempts his first plant introduction without authorization or funding (the Secretary of Agriculture had refused both); he is briefly arrested before sneaking four citron bud-sticks into his coat pocket and escaping the island
Fairchild travelled to Java to study termites at the botanical gardens in Buitenzorg (now Bogor).
Fairchild and Lathrop visit Sumatra together
Fairchild visits Australia during Pacific tour with Lathrop, notes eucalyptus as a potential plant introduction.

Fairchild visits Fiji with Lathrop. There, he tries kava, the juice of the root of the Piper methysticum plant; which is used for cultural and recreational purposes in Oceania. In traditional medicine, kava is used to treat anxiety and stress.
Fairchild returns to Washington and co-founds the Office of Seed and Plant Introduction with Walter Swingle.
Fairchild and Lathrop begin their second world tour by collecting chayote and mangoes in Jamaica.

In Santiago, Fairchild packs nearly 1,000  avocados and ships more than 10 boxes to Washington; This Chilean variety,which is desired for its consistent size and frost tolerance down to 23°F, would become a founding ancestor of the California avocado industry and ultimately the Hass avocado.
Fairchild and Lathrop crossed the Andes while making their way through Argentina, collecting samples of the bella dona tree (which is now a popular shade tree in many parts of the US).
Fairchild collects and ships Itamaraca mango
At a London market Fairchild picks up Windsor broad beans (fava beans) and ships them back to Washington.

Dining alone in Venice, Fairchild tries broccoli for the first time and pays the café's chef for a handful of seeds along with sweet peppers; notably, Walter Swingle had already shipped a primitive form from France months earlier; Fairchild was not the official first introducer of broccoli to the US
Fairchild collects sultanina (Thompson) seedless grapes at Padua.

Crop: Egyptian cotton (Jannovitch); sesame; garbanzo beans; berseem clover
Event: Staying at Cairo's Shepheard's Hotel, Fairchild assembles his largest shipment to date, totalling 43 crop types including Jannovitch long-fiber cotton (that would help improve a southwestern American cotton industry worth $100 million by WWII), sesame, garbanzo beans, and berseem clover
While cruising the Malay Islands, Fairchild collects a pomelo, one of Earth's few original citrus ancestors (Citrus maxima), from the island of Sekar.
Fairchild collects the carabao mango on the outskirts of Manila which would later be marketed as the champagne mango.
Fairchild collects rice varieties, water chestnuts, and guava in Canton.

After befriending hop growers in Bohemia, Fairchild covertly takes 100 cuttings of the prized Semš hop variety; they arrive in America on December 18 and eventually help the US brewing industry surpass European rivals, aided further by WWI devastating Europe's hop fields.
Fairchild collects grape varieties, carob and hazelnuts in Italy.

Along the Austrian coast, Fairchild encounters capuzzo, a two-thousand-year-old leafy cabbage the rest of the world would call kale; he ships it for trial in the American South, kale would go on to become a nutritional phenomenon a century later.

Alongside the German botanist Theodor von Heldreich, Fairchild bought fully grown pistachio trees. While the American public was already familiar with pistachios, these were the first to be imported, so they became the first pistachio trees in America. He also shipped lentils and seedless lemon varieties from the island of Poros.
In Bombay, Fairchild shipped a gigantic collection of around 80 varieties of mango, including the highly prized Alphonso variety. These were sent to Florida for testing.

Fairchild attempts to collect mangosteen in Saigon, the fruit he considered the best in the world; he also secures Saigon mango seed; despite lifelong efforts he never succeeded in establishing mangosteen in American soil.

Fairchild travels to Baghdad and collects date palm offshoots and dryland staples from Mesopotamia, the birthplace of agriculture; the date palms would help establish the date industry in the American Southwest.
Fairchild collects nectarines in Quetta (then British India).

Crop: Wasabi; loquat; zoysia grass; persimmon; flowering cherry trees; udo
Event: Fairchild visits Japan and collects a wide range of edible and landscape plants; his first encounter with flowering cherry trees plants the seed for what will become one of his most lasting contributions, the iconic cherry blossoms lining the Tidal Basin in Washington D.C., gifted by Japan in 1912.

Crop: Kaffir orange; carissa plum; miniature pineapple
Event: Fairchild and Lathrop embark on their last major botanical expedition together, circumnavigating Africa; they collect Kaffir orange, carissa plum, and a miniature pineapple before Fairchild returns to Washington in August 1903.
Fairchild marries Marian Bell, daughter of inventor Alexander Graham Bell; the couple met through the National Geographic Society in 1903.
Frank Meyer hired by Fairchild after interview at USDA office.
Frank Meyer departs for first China expedition (3 years); his first assignment was to find grindstone persimmon in Beijing.

Meyer discovers the "Meyer" lemon near Beijing, a citrus hybrid combining the sweetness of a mandarin with the tartness of a lemon; the fruit he ships back will bear his name and become popular in America.

Crop: Oats; millet; watermelon; cotton; wild pears; persimmons; Manchurian spinach; asparagus; soybeans; bamboo (30 types); red blackberries; wild apricots; zelkova trees; Chinese holly; white-barked pines; lilacs (18 forms); viburnum; spirea; rhododendron; daphne; lilies; lawn sedge
Event: During this expedition, Meyer walks more than 20 miles a dat across China, Manchuria, Korea, and eastern Siberia over 3 years. Sends first soybeans and asparagus to officially enter the US. Returns in 1908 with 20 tons of plant material.

Crop: Brazilian cherries; cashew wine; guava jelly; jaboticaba
Event: Wilson Popenoe departs for Brazil in search of a superior navel orange; he returns with Brazilian cherries, cashew wine, guava jelly, and jaboticaba grapes.

Crop: Walnuts; hazelnuts; chestnuts (blight resistant); jujubes; pears; apples; peaches; haw
Event: Meyer third expedition: discovers Chinese chestnut trees resistant to blight devastating American chestnuts.
Wilson Popenoe spends three years riding thousands of miles on horseback through Guatemala, studying thousands of avocado trees and living on beans and chayotes.
The Fairchilds buy a property in Coconut Grove on Biscayne Bay; Lathrop names it the Kampong and it would later become the National Tropical Botanical Garde.

Meyer's fourth and final expedition; suffering severe mental breakdown amid WWI-era chaos and rebellion in China, he makes one last collection, ginkgo biloba seeds and Ichang lemons, before dying under sad circumstances in the Yangtze River valley.
First Frank Meyer Medal for plant introduction awarded to Barbour Lathrop.
During his last official plant-hunting expedition, Fairchild visited Venezuela and Colombia. He brought back accessions of bamboos, annonas, palms, arracacha and corozo with him to the US.

David Fairchild dies on August 6 at the Kampong in Coconut Grove under a Javanese ficus tree, at age 85; over his career he introduced more than 200,000 plant species and varieties to the United States.
